claim: The extremely high hapax legomena ratio (70.1% of 8,261 unique word types) is generated by a systematic scribal abbreviation convention â€” specifically, word-final glyph truncation â€” rather than representing a genuinely large underlying vocabulary, such that reconstructing truncated forms by appending the statistically most probable word-final glyph to hapax tokens would reduce the hapax ratio to below 50% and increase Zipf fit RÂ².
evidence_for: Word-final glyphs y, n, l, r, o dominate, consistent with a small set of permitted endings that could be systematically dropped
A truncation/abbreviation convention would create the appearance of vocabulary explosion from a compact base vocabulary
Medieval scribal abbreviation was ubiquitous and period-appropriate
Belief [0.05] supports suffix-stripping as an artifactual source of high hapax ratio
evidence_against: Current Zipf RÂ²=0.9084 is already moderate, suggesting real frequency structure; truncation artifacts typically degrade Zipf fit rather than preserve it
If truncation were systematic, we would expect clusters of hapax tokens differing only in final glyph â€” this pattern has not been confirmed in available statistics
High hapax ratio is consistent with a homophonic or polyalphabetic cipher producing many surface word-forms from fewer underlying forms
test: For all hapax tokens, compute Levenshtein distance to non-hapax (frequency â‰¥ 2) word types. If truncation is the mechanism, hapax tokens should cluster within edit distance 1 of higher-frequency words with the difference being a single word-final glyph. Measure the fraction of hapax tokens within edit distance 1 of a non-hapax token versus the null expectation from random glyph sequences of matching length distribution.
test_metric: Fraction of hapax tokens with a non-hapax neighbor at Levenshtein distance 1 differing only at the final position
test_threshold: Fraction > 0.35 (more than 35% of hapax tokens are truncated variants of attested words) would support systematic abbreviation; fraction < 0.15 would refute it relative to a random baseline of ~0.08

tests_run.details: Observed hapax ratio: 0.701. Expected: ~0.50. Voynich has unusually high hapax ratio (70.1%), suggesting either large vocabulary, verbose encoding, or noise.

reasoning: If confirmed, this hypothesis changes the effective vocabulary size from 8,261 to potentially under 4,000 types, dramatically constraining both the cipher space and the underlying language identification
